---
title: "Research"
pagetitle: "Research — Bingjin Xue"
---

## Published and Forthcoming

::: {.paper}
[Tourism Destinations in Crisis: Economic Ripple Effects]{.title}

[with Hanna Lee, Yifei Wang, and Jinwon Kim]{.authors}

[*Journal of Hospitality and Tourism Management*, 2026, 67, 101437.]{.venue}

[Can a single act of violence disrupt an entire regional tourism market? We use the 2018 Parkland school shooting as a localized safety shock and a triple-difference design on 419,057 Florida Airbnb listings to trace how perceived safety propagates through supply, demand, and host listing behavior in platform-mediated tourism markets.]{.abstract}

<div class="actions">
[Published version](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhtm.2026.101437)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Bittersweet: Grandparenting and Elderly Mental Health in the Two-Child Policy Era]{.title}

[with Dapeng Chen and Shin-Yi Chou]{.authors}

[*Journal of Development Economics*, 2025, 103670.]{.venue}

[Does grandparenting improve or erode elderly mental health? Using China's 2016 universal two-child policy as a shock to grandchild arrivals, we find that the resulting rise in grandparental involvement raises CESD-10 depression scores by 6.8% and the frequency of symptoms — restless sleep, irritability — by 8.9% to 14.3%. Effects concentrate in urban grandparents and in paternal grandparents, especially paternal grandmothers. We find no evidence of elevated severe clinical depression.]{.abstract}

<div class="actions">
[Published version](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jdeveco.2025.103670)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Implications of the Decline in LGBT Rights for Population Mental Health: Evidence from Polish "LGBT-free Zones"]{.title}

[with Chad D. Meyerhoefer and Anna Poznańska]{.authors}

[*Journal of Health Economics*, 2025, 100, 102973.]{.venue}

[Do anti-LGBT policies harm population mental health? Using Polish "LGBT-free zone" adoptions in 2019–2020 and a difference-in-differences design against bordering non-adopting counties, we find suicide attempts rose 16.5%, suicide deaths rose 17.0%, and deaths from external causes rose 10.6%. The rise in attempts concentrates among ages 13–18 and 45–49, and coincides with an 8.8% increase in prescription sales for addictive-disorder drugs.]{.abstract}

<div class="actions">
[Published version](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhealeco.2025.102973) ·
[NBER version](https://www.nber.org/papers/w31702)
</div>
:::

## Under Review

::: {.paper}
[Abortion, Economic Hardship, and Crime]{.title}

[with Erkmen G. Aslim, Wei Fu, Caitlin Myers, and Erdal Tekin]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag} NBER Working Paper 34245]{.venue}

[Do abortion restrictions fuel financially motivated crime? Using variation in travel distance to Texas abortion providers generated by post-HB-2 (2013) clinic closures, we show that reduced access causes lower labor force participation, rising debt, widening income inequality, and housing insecurity. This financial strain translates into higher rates of theft and burglary, with no detectable effect on violent crime.]{.abstract}

<div class="actions">
[NBER working paper](https://www.nber.org/papers/w34245) ·
[SSRN](https://ssrn.com/abstract=5487070)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[The Effects of Switching Electronic Health Record Developer on Specialty Referrals]{.title}

[with Chad D. Meyerhoefer]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag} NBER Working Paper 33861]{.venue}

[When a hospital switches electronic health record (EHR) developer, how do its affiliated primary care physicians reroute specialty referrals? Using a difference-in-differences design across EHR switches, we find referrals to same-developer specialists rise 5.8% and referrals to specialists on the prior EHR fall 4.2%. A theoretical model shows PCP preferences for convenience can route patients away from the highest-quality specialists and fuel EHR-market agglomeration — an economic rationale for antitrust regulation of information blocking.]{.abstract}

<div class="actions">
[NBER working paper](https://www.nber.org/papers/w33861) ·
[SSRN](https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5278364)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[From Overdose Prevention to Reproductive Health: The Impact of Naloxone Access Laws]{.title}

[with Dajung Jun]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

:::

::: {.paper}
[Unlocking Creativity: Public Health Insurance Provision and Artists' Labor Market Outcomes]{.title}

[with Kevin S. Smith]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

:::

::: {.paper}
[The Impact of E-Cigarette Regulations on Birth Outcomes]{.title}

[with Yizhao Wang]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

:::

::: {.paper}
[Design on Top of Design]{.title}

[single-authored]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

:::

## Working Papers

::: {.paper}
[Naloxone Coprescription Laws: Expanding Safety Without Fueling Misuse]{.title}

[with Shin-Yi Chou and Jiarun Li]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

:::

::: {.paper}
[Pressure from Students' Peer Parents]{.title}

[with Dapeng Chen and Junjian Yi]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

:::

::: {.paper}
[Gender Gap in Response to Work Environment Changes: The Impact of EHR Switching on Physician Referral Networks]{.title}

[single-authored]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

:::

::: {.paper}
[Habit Formation in the Consumption of Healthcare Services]{.title}

[with Chad D. Meyerhoefer]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

:::
